claim: For all integers n, gcd(n^2+1, n+1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+1, n+1) equals 2 iff n is odd, and 1 iff n is even.
n^2+1 is never divisible by 4.
n+1 and n^2+1 are both even exactly when n is odd.
If n is odd, gcd(n^2+1,n+1)=2 and (n^2+1)/2 and (n+1)/2 are not both even.
gcd(n^2+1, n-1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+1,n-1) equals 2 iff n is odd, and 1 iff n is even.
gcd(n^2+1, 2n+1) divides 5.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2-1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+1,n^2-1) equals 2 iff n is odd.
For n odd, (n^2+1)/2 is never divisible by 4.
n^2+1 is divisible by 5 iff n is congruent to 2 or 3 mod 5.
n^2+1 has no prime factor congruent to 3 mod 4.

If n^2+1 is divisible by 5 then n^2+1 is divisible by 5 but never by 25 when n is not congruent to 0 mod 5 in a specific residual pattern (n mod 25 in {2,3,7,8,12,13,17,18,22,23} gives exactly one factor of 5).
gcd(n^2+1,4) never equals 4.
gcd(n^2+1, 2(n+1)) divides 4.

n^3+1 and n+1 have gcd equal to n+1 when n is not -2 mod small primes, in general gcd(n^3+1,n+1)=n+1 unless n+1 divides 3 exactly... specifically gcd(n^3+1,n+1) equals n+1 always since n+1 divides n^3+1.
gcd(n^3+1, n^2+1) divides 2 times gcd(n+1,n^2+1)/gcd(n+1,n^2+1) i.e. gcd(n^3+1,n^2+1) divides n^2 - n +1 factor structure, concretely gcd(n^3+1,n^2+1) divides 2.
gcd(n^2+n+1, n+1) equals 1 for all integers n.
gcd(n^2+n+1, n^2+1) divides n, hence divides gcd(n,n^2+1) which divides 1.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2-n+1) divides n(n-2), and in fact this gcd always divides 2 as well.
For n even, both gcd(n^2+1,n+1) and gcd(n^2+1,n-1) equal 1 simultaneously.
For n odd, gcd(n+1,n-1,n^2+1) as a triple gcd equals 2.
n^2+1 is squarefree with respect to the prime 2 and prime 5 cannot both divide it to higher power simultaneously in a way that forces 20 | n^2+1... more precisely, n^2+1 is never divisible by 20.

n^2+1 is never divisible by 10 for n even, but can be divisible by 10 for suitable odd n... actually n^2+1 divisible by 10 requires n odd and n = 2,3 mod5, giving exact characterization: (n^2+1)%10==0 iff n%10 in {3,7}.
phi(n^2+1) is even for all n>2.
tau(n^2+1) is even for all n>0, i.e. n^2+1 is never a perfect square for positive n (except n=0).
n^2+1 is a perfect square only when n=0.
sigma(n^2+1) is divisible by 6 whenever n^2+1 is divisible by 5 and n is odd.
gcd(n^3+1,n^2+1) equals 2 iff n is odd, and 1 iff n is even.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2+n+1, n+1) triple gcd equals 1 for all n.
For n odd, gcd(n^2+1, n^2-1) equals gcd(n+1,n-1,n^2+1) i.e. both triple structures give exactly 2.
vp(n^2+1,2) is at most 1 for all n.
For n odd, phi(n^2+1) is divisible by 2*vp(n^2+1,2) trivially since vp<=1, so phi(n^2+1) even is consistent, and moreover (n^2+1)/2 is odd or even depending on n mod 4.
n^2+1 divisible by 5 implies n^2+1 not divisible by 4, hence gcd(n^2+1,20) divides 5 in that case is trivial, but more precisely n^2+1 divisible by 5 and n odd implies (n^2+1)/2 divisible by 5.
gcd(n^2+1,2n+1) equals 5 iff n congruent to 2 mod5, and 1 otherwise (for the relevant residues where 2n+1 shares factor).
The sequence gcd(n^2+1,n+1) for n=0..k alternates strictly between 1 and 2 with period 2 in n.
The sequence gcd(n^2+1,n-1) also has period 2 in n.
n^4+1 is never a perfect square for n != 0.
tau(n^4+1) is even for all n>0.
gcd(n^2+1, n^2+2) equals 1 for all n (consecutive integers are coprime).
gcd(n^2+2, n^2-1) divides 3 for all n.
For n odd, (n^2+1)/2 is odd iff n congruent to 1 mod 4.
For n congruent to 3 mod 4, (n^2+1)/2 is even, hence divisible by exactly one factor of 2 beyond the base (i.e. vp((n^2+1)/2, 2)=1).

For all n, at most one of n^2+1 and n+1 is divisible by an odd prime p that also divides the other, unless p divides 2, tying back to the gcd bound of 2 — restated as: gcd(n^2+1,n+1) is never divisible by any odd prime.
gcd(n^2+1, n+1) is a power of 2 for all n (specifically 1 or 2).
For all n, gcd(n^2+1,n+1)*gcd(n^2+1,n-1) equals gcd(n^2+1,n^2-1) when n is odd, and both trivial factors 1 when n is even, i.e. product identity holds for the gcd triple.
For n congruent to 1 mod 4, vp(n^2+1,2) equals 1 and (n^2+1)/2 is odd, making n^2+1 congruent to 2 mod 4.
For n congruent to 3 mod 4, n^2+1 is congruent to 2 mod 4 as well (same conclusion as the 1 mod 4 case via symmetry).
For all odd n, n^2+1 is congruent to 2 mod 4, unifying both residue classes mod 4.
For all odd n>1, n^2+1 has at least two distinct prime factors (is not a prime power of an odd prime, nor twice a prime power beyond the single factor of 2).
predicate: (n%2==0 or n<=1) or (len(set([p for p in range(2,n**2+2) if (n**2+1)%p==0 and is_prime(p)]))>=2 or (n**2+1)==2)
